data.title: ECB Leaves For The Summer With A Touch Of Doris Day
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

After seven consecutive 25bp rate cuts and a total of eight and 200bp rate cuts since September 2023, the European Central Bank kept its policy interest rates on hold at today’s meeting. At the press conference, ECB president Christine Lagarde kept all options open, but it looks as if the bar for more rate cuts is gradually increasing

No changes to the ECB's macro assessment

The ECB’s macro-economic assessment has not changed since the last meeting. The ECB still sees a resilient eurozone economy, though risks to the growth outlook remain tilted to the downside. Lagarde made explicit what these downside risks were: a further escalation in global trade tensions, geopolitical tensions, and a deterioration in financial market sentiment. As regards inflation, the ECB still refrains from gauging the risk assessment. Instead, the ECB has again labelled the outlook for inflation as more uncertain than usual.

Concerns about stronger euro and inflation undershooting were downplayed

Lagarde quickly dodged any questions on the stronger euro, stressing the expected wording that the ECB had no exchange rate target and was only looking at the exchange rate as part of the transmission mechanism. Shortly after the June meeting, the stronger euro exchange rate had given rise to speculations on the continuation of the current rate cut cycle in July.

As regards the potential risk of inflation undershooting, as a result of a stronger euro, Lagarde played down the risk by pointing to the fact that the ECB’s June staff projections had already predicted a minor inflation undershooting over the coming months. According to Lagarde, a minor inflation undershooting did not pose a problem, as it was the medium-term outlook that mattered for monetary policy.

ECB feels comfortable in the 'good place' but one final rate cut still possible

All in all, with a deposit rate of 2%, headline inflation at 2% and an inflation outlook that fulfills the old ‘below, but close to 2%’, the ECB can simply wait for who is holding the better trade cards in the trade negotiations and whether actual inflation will really behave as predicted. Or as Christine Lagarde put it: the ECB remains data-dependent and follows a meeting-by-meeting approach, focusing on the inflation outlook and risks surrounding it, dynamics of underlying inflation and the strength of monetary policy transmission. As long as core and services inflation remain above 2% and the fiscal stimulus story in Germany and the rest of the eurozone remains intact, there is very little reason for it to leave its ‘good place’. However, if the trade tensions are resolved quickly and a lifting of uncertainty increases resilience of the eurozone economy, the debate at the ECB could quickly shift - from whether or not more rate cuts are needed to when to hike rates in order to tackle inflationary pressures stemming from fiscal stimulus.

Admittedly, taking today’s meeting at face value, the bar for yet another rate cut this year has clearly been raised. Still, we think that actual inflation could come in lower than the ECB expects and hard macro data could rather disappoint over the summer. This, together with the fact that the ECB’s June projections actually included a terminal rate of 1.75%, still encourages us to see one final rate cut at the September meeting. But our conviction has become weaker today.

Christine Lagarde did not give away any hints on what could be next for the ECB, simply reiterating in several ways that everything is possible. To give today’s ECB meeting a more entertaining note, the ECB today could have also turned on the old song by Doris Day ‘Que sera sera’. It’s the ECB’s summer theme.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Alphabet: The Most Important Earnings Number
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Alphabet Inc.’s (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Q2 2025 was another blowout quarter, and the market can’t ignore it anymore.

Every segment is growing healthily, cloud is killing it, and Google is doubling down on AI, with an $85 billion investment.

There’s not much to dislike right now when it comes to Google, and the valuation is still among the most compelling in the big tech sector.

I emphasize my Strong Buy rating on Google, which is back to being my favorite stock.

Q2 Overview: What’s Not To Like

Alphabet just reported $96.4B in revenue, up 14% YoY and beating expectations of $94B.

Meanwhile, Net income climbed 19% and reached $28.2B, with EPS at $2.31, beating by $0.13, and Operating margin held steady at 32.4%.

Now, let’s break this down by segments.

Google

Despite all the concerns around Search, this area is still growing, up almost 10% QoQ. AI-driven features have actually contributed towards boosting query volume, according to management.

We also saw strong growth in Google Lens searches, up 70% YoY.

YouTube & Network

YouTube ads grew 13% YoY, with shorts now the main engine of growth. Shorts received an average of 200 billion daily views and are beginning to catch up to long-form videos in terms of monetization.

Google Network, which includes AdSense, AdMob, and Google Ad Manager, fell 1% YoY.

Subscriptions & Devices

This sub-segment grew over 20% thanks to an increase in YouTube subscriptions and Google One, which now includes AI Pro and Ultra plans tied to Gemini.

Google Cloud

Very bullish news for Google Cloud, which continues to fire on all cylinders and reported $13.6B in revenues, a 32% YoY increase and well above consensus.

There’s also a backlog of $106 billion, which is up 38% YoY.

Other Bets (Waymo)

And then we have other bets, which lost over $1.2 billion. This is nothing to Google, though, and within this segment, Waymo still looks very promising, with the company now completing over 100 million autonomous miles and expanding to 10 US cities.

Management actually said they’d be allocating more funds to Waymo going forward.

The Most Important Number From The Earnings Call

But undoubtedly, the biggest news was the announcement that Google would be spending $85 billion in CAPEX in order to further its AI agenda.

Google already spent over $22 billion in Q2 in CAPEX, and the total number for the year will be as high as $85 billion.

Google is not just resting on its laurels, and it could, it’s going all in on AI, and this time around, investors are pleased.

Insights From The Earnings Call

But the numbers only really tell half the story. In order to understand the success of Google’s business and its thoughts on AI, we must dive into the earnings call.

On Cloud’s Success:

We operate the leading global network of AI-optimized data centers and offer the industry’s widest range of TPUs and GPUs… nearly all GenAI unicorns use Google Cloud.

Source: Earnings Call.

Google has unique offerings when it comes to cloud, and is differentiated by its TPUs and GPUs. Over 85,000 enterprises now build using Gemini and that is driving growth.

Agentic AI Is The Next Frontier:

So the forward-looking trajectory, I think, will really unlock these agentic experiences. We see the potential of being able to do them, but they're a bit slow and costly and takes time, and sometimes are brittle, right? But they're making progress on all of that. And I think that's what will really unlock. And I expect 2026 to be the year in which people kind of use agentic experiences more broadly, right? And so it's an exciting opportunity ahead.

Source: Earnings Call.

It’s about taking AI to the next level and making it proactive, rather than reactive. This is key to understanding, because agentic AI will touch all of Google’s businesses. In fact, it already is, and it is already having a very positive effect on the business.

On the increased Capex:

It’s a tight supply environment… We’re investing now to expand capacity, but there’s a delay before this plays out.

Source: Earnings Call.

Google is anticipating increased demand for AI spend and is expanding capacity before it reaches a bottleneck. Bear in mind, Cloud has a backlog of over $100 billion.

My 2 Cents

All in all, it’s hard to deny that Google is doing very well on all fronts, even with Search. And they can’t be faulted for not trying their best to innovate.

The way I see it, this is a perfect example of creative destruction. Yes, AI may eventually destroy some of Google's business, but at least the company is having the foresight to do it itself, instead of letting the market do it.

The P/E is fair, and the PEG is under 1. There’s really not much to complain about when it comes to Google right now, but I’ll try my best to play devil's advocate.

Risks

Two main risks with Google right now.

Firstly, it is important to note that cash flow turned negative this quarter. Something to be expected given the large investments the company is making, but understandably, this may worry some investors.

On top of that, Google is still under the eye of regulators, with the UK being the latest to question whether Google has too much power. This is an ever-present risk with Google, but one that hasn’t actually been a substantial setback.

Final Thoughts

All in all, a great quarter, and it’s not surprising Google is up on the news and also rallied into the evening.

AI is actually helping the business, even search, and the market still has some work to do to give Google the valuation it deserves, in my opinion.

data.title: SAP: Increasingly Difficult To Justify Premium As Growth Slows (Downgrade)
data.description:
With the S&P 500 skyrocketing to fresh new highs, investors concerned about overvalued markets should look first to aggressive rallies in the large-cap tech sector as the core standard-bearer of overinflated multiples. While it has become increasingly difficult to resist the notion that tech is the only winning trade amid a consumer sector that has been weighed by weaker consumer spending and the threat of tariffs, investors should take care to position their portfolios carefully in the very likely event of a correction in valuation multiples.

For SAP (NYSE:SAP), that correction may already be beginning. The German software giant recently reported Q2 results, recording a rare miss to Wall Street's expectations that has knocked ~5% off the stock. While SAP remains up nearly 20% year to date, well outpacing the S&P 500, I suspect that its momentum may be beginning to break.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on SAP in April, when the stock was trading in the $270s. Since then, SAP has rallied alongside the broader market: but at the same time, we're worried about the company's slowing growth amid extended valuation multiples and an uncertain global macroeconomic environment. While I certainly recommend diversifying beyond U.S. stocks and buying relatively cheaper European stocks, "cheap" is the operative word here—and SAP is anything but cheap. With the stock's valuation premium looking excessively larger as U.S. peers like Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE) decline, I'm downgrading the stock to a "sell" rating.

Beyond valuation, here's what I consider to be the core red flags that SAP faces on a fundamental basis:

  • A saturated global brand with few greenfield market opportunities remaining. SAP has a deep portfolio in enterprise software that already touches almost all areas of IT, ranging from front-end applications for CRM/sales and procurement to backend ERP systems. SAP is already one of the global gold standards when it comes to ERP. Slowing growth, in my view, is a signal that SAP is starting to become a victim of its own size.
  • Headwinds from a very strong euro. The euro was at one point nearly on par with the dollar in early 2025, but now that the euro has soared (both due to increased interest in European investments as Germany steps up military spending and as the dollar itself declines), SAP has suffered a headwind as it re-rates its guidance down based on a $1.17 euro-dollar exchange rate.
  • Rapid change with AI may upend the industry. SAP was relatively late to migrate its clients to the cloud when companies like Workday (WDAY) and Salesforce were rising. Though the company has certainly released AI products and features, it may be behind in innovations like agentic AI (which, among large-caps, companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Salesforce may be leading in). SAP may be at risk of losing its leadership position, particularly in ERP, if it is outgunned in its AI advances.

The biggest blow to SAP, however, is the fact that expectations for the stock are already so heady, which has led to over-bloated valuation multiples.

As shown in the chart above, SAP's 40.6x forward P/E ratio towers over several other large-cap enterprise software peers, and its 7.7x forward revenue multiple is only outgunned by Microsoft (which is anyway largely valued on an earnings basis as a very mature and profitable software company). To me, Workday is a particularly compelling and worrying comparison for SAP, as Workday is growing at a slightly faster pace than SAP (13% growth in its most recent quarter) and is trading at a ~30% cheaper P/E multiple. To me, there is very little that justifies SAP's premium, and based on peer multiples, I think there is ~30% or more for SAP to fall based on valuation alone.

If you've been fortunate to hold SAP through its meteoric ~40% rally over the past year, I'd take the opportunity now to lock in gains.

Q2 Download

Let's now go through SAP's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

SAP's revenue grew just 9% y/y to €9.03 billion. Here, the company logged a three-point headwind from currency: on a constant currency basis, revenue growth would have been three points stronger at 12% y/y.

As usual, all eyes are on the company's cloud revenue growth, which is now over half of overall revenue. The company's cloud revenue of €5.13 billion (+28% y/y in constant currency terms) in the quarter drove mixed reactions: on the one hand, it accelerated 200bps on a constant currency basis versus 26% growth in Q1, but at the same time, Street estimates had hoped for slightly higher €5.18 billion in cloud revenue (about 120bps stronger in terms of y/y growth).

Again, we have to emphasize here that while SAP's ~30% cloud growth appears very robust, this is mainly a function of the company's rather late transition to the cloud and is coming at the expense of weaker license sales.

We note as well that the company's growth rate in current cloud backlog decelerated by one point, which the company attributed to elongating sales cycles in the U.S.

Per CFO Dominik Asam's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

As we look towards half year 2, we are mindful of the broader environment, including in geopolitical developments, notably the ongoing uncertainty about trade policy that has contributed to elongated sales cycles in certain sectors, such as U.S. public sector and industrial and manufacturing. The sequential 1 percentage point deceleration in current cloud backlog growth is underscoring the dampening effect on bookings in Q2. It is obviously hard, if not impossible, to predict when exactly we'll catch up on the pushouts. Closing these open opportunities will be a focus in half year 2, where we, as you will recall, usually close roughly 2/3 of our annual new cloud business.

Unfortunately, we have no crystal ball to reliably predict global trade policy decision-making. And it goes without saying that the longer this uncertainty persists, the more pressure it is likely to put on global trade and our customers' ability to make well-informed decisions.

Where SAP is comparatively shining, however, is in its margin expansion. The company's pro forma operating margins rose 500 bps y/y to 28.5%. Unlike many other software companies that are simply touting the benefits of AI and automation only to their customers, SAP is dedicated to deploying AI to "reskill" its workforce and rethink its pace of hiring. As a result, as shown in the chart below, the company's R&D spending as a percentage of revenue fell -140bps y/y to 17.9%, while sales and marketing expenses also fell -280bps y/y to 23.1%, driving the bulk of the operating margin gains.

Key Takeaways

SAP's appeal is waning, with a strong euro weighing against its growth and stacking on top of weak U.S. sales cycles. The stock's ~5% post-earnings decline and SAP's own inability to lift its full-year guidance, in my view, mark the beginning of a stiff devaluation in SAP stock that will bring its overbloated valuation multiples more in line with peers. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Alphabet's Q2 Beat Proves It's Way Too Cheap
data.description:
Article Thesis

On Wednesday evening, Alphabet Inc. aka Google (GOOG, GOOGL) reported strong quarterly earnings results. These reaffirm my belief that Alphabet is well-positioned for the future, benefiting from strong growth in several areas. At the same time, Alphabet trades at an undemanding valuation, which is why I remain very bullish on this company -- in my eyes, it's the best Magnificent 7 stock to own right now.

Past Coverage

I have written about Alphabet Inc. here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently three months ago, when I covered Alphabet's Q1 earnings report. I was pretty bullish back then, which has worked out well so far, as GOOG returned 15% in just one quarter. With Alphabet updating us on its financial performance on Wednesday, it is time to take another look at the company and to update my thesis on this AI winner.

What Happened?

On Wednesday afternoon, Alphabet released its second-quarter earnings results. These were the headline numbers:

The company beat the analyst consensus estimate on both lines, which wasn't much of a surprise -- the company has done so in 9 out of the last 10 quarters, so GOOG beating the Wall Street consensus is almost tradition. Still, the results were quite compelling, as the revenue beat was in the multi-billion dollar range, and since the earnings per share estimate was outperformed by more than 5%, this wasn't a just-above-estimates situation.

Alphabet: A Strong Performance Across Different Business Units

Let's delve into the numbers: Alphabet's revenue growth, at 14%, was broad-based, with the company showing compelling momentum across different business units. Google Search, its biggest unit in terms of revenue generation, at a little more than half of the company's overall revenue, generated a revenue growth rate of 12%, which is quite compelling, I believe, for a business that is generating more than $200 billion in revenue on an annualized basis. The double-digit revenue increase for the core search business also suggests that worries about AI technologies such as ChatGPT replacing the more traditional web search business seem overblown. If that were the case and ChatGPT, Grok, and so on were to encroach on Google Search's business in a big way, Google Search wouldn't show strong revenue growth, I believe.

The strong revenue growth rate for Google Search is thus not only positive for the company's overall financial results, but it can also be seen as a sign that business disruption fears are overblown. Of course, there is still the possibility that Google Search will peak out at some point in the future, but at least for now, that's not the case, as the business unit continues to grow at a very nice pace.

Other business units performed well, too, with YouTube's ad business showing a double-digit growth rate as well. Revenues rose from $8.7 billion to $9.8 billion, with YouTube now generating almost $40 billion in annualized revenue purely from its advertisements. YouTube remains highly relevant, especially for younger audiences, and seems to be well-positioned compared to competing offers from other platforms.

Google Cloud saw its revenues rise by more than 30%, which is a very attractive growth rate in relative terms, especially when we consider the unit's large absolute size: Google Cloud generates more than $50 billion in revenue on a per-year basis, meaning the unit adds billions of dollars in revenue per quarter at the current growth rate.

Alphabet's Other Bets revenue rose only slightly, but is relatively negligible anyway, at a couple of hundred million dollars per year, relative to the company's overall revenue generation.

All in all, Alphabet's business growth rate looks quite appealing, I believe: Google Cloud is growing at a very strong pace, while other key franchises such as YouTube and Google Search continue to expand at a double-digit pace as well.

Looking at the cost side, the first item of importance is Alphabet's traffic acquisition cost. That number rose by around $1.3 billion, which is around 10% of the previous year's total. Traffic acquisition costs thus seem to grow a little slower than Google Search's revenue (a large part of the company's traffic acquisition cost is related to making people use Google as a search engine), which means that Google Search's take rate is expanding. Google Search's revenue growth in absolute terms, at $6.7 billion, was also way ahead of the $1.5 billion increase in traffic acquisition costs. Since the traffic acquisition cost increase was more than outpaced by revenue growth, Google Services, which includes Google Search, YouTube ads, and so on, grew its profit by a little more than 10% compared to one year earlier.

Google Cloud saw a way stronger profit growth rate, more than doubling its operating profit from one year earlier, with that number rising from $1.17 billion to $2.83 billion over the last year. This not only means that Google Cloud is now generating more than $10 billion per year in profit, but the massive growth rate also indicates that the unit benefits a lot from operating leverage: With some of its expenses being fixed, a nice pace of revenue growth translates into an even better earnings growth rate. I thus believe that Google Cloud will be a major source of profit growth for Alphabet in the future, at least as long as its revenue growth rate remains significant -- which will, I assume, hold true for many years, thanks to market growth tailwinds and Google's strong brand and market position.

Alphabet lost more money in its Other Bets business, where losses rose by a little more than $100 million. This is likely due to increased investments in businesses such as Waymo, which is rolling out its robotaxi service in more and more cities and which recently began mapping in Philadelphia. While Waymo isn't profitable for now, I still like the fact that Alphabet has exposure to the robotaxi market. Success isn't certain, of course, but Waymo seems to be in the leadership position for now, and if the robotaxi market turns as large and profitable as some believe, then Waymo could become an attractive profit source for Alphabet 5 or 10 years from now. And if Waymo isn't successful or it turns out that robotaxi market projections were too bullish, then that's not dramatic for Alphabet, either -- the company doesn't depend on success in the robotaxi world, unlike its Magnificent 7 peer Tesla (TSLA).

In the conference call, Alphabet stated that it plans to spend heavily on capital expenditures this year, with the capex forecast standing at a hefty $85 billion. Some investors may dislike this fact, as it is not certain that all of these investments, e.g., in data center capacity, will pay off. But Alphabet has proven very competent at identifying future growth markets in the past, it has a track record of making new units highly profitable eventually (e.g., Google Cloud), and it generates ample cash flows, so I don't see the hefty capex spending as a problem.

Investors who are more uncertain about management's ability to deliver may disagree with me, however. Alphabet has generated around $63 billion in operating cash flow so far this year, which is why I believe that the company could generate around $130 billion this year in total, which would result in a $45 billion free cash flow after accounting for Alphabet's massive spending plans. -- the dividend is thus very secure, and Alphabet has additional firepower for buybacks, M&A, and so on.

Alphabet: The Most Attractive Magnificent 7 Company

I believe that the Q2 report shows that worries about the Search business are overblown -- revenues and profits keep getting better and better. YouTube is strong, Google Cloud is very strong, Alphabet is among the leaders in AI and robotaxis, and yet, it is a pretty inexpensive stock. Based on current earnings per share estimates for this year (which should get raised following the Q2 double beat), Alphabet trades at 20x net profits right now, making it the cheapest Magnificent 7 stock by far. Considering that it's not the slowest-growing of these at all -- Tesla is declining, Apple (AAPL) is barely growing, and Amazon (AMZN) will likely report a weaker growth rate than GOOG as well next week -- I think that Alphabet is the best Magnificent 7 pick to buy today.

data.title: Natural Gas Price Weakens On Record Output And Soft Demand (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

U.S. natural gas futures are under pressure, trading near $3.096 per million British thermal units (MMBtu) as of July 24, marking the lowest price since late April. Traders are reacting to updated weather forecasts showing weaker-than-expected heat across the U.S., alongside continued record-breaking production.

These developments have combined to weaken bullish expectations, despite strong LNG export levels. Technically, natural gas broke below a key ascending triangle structure earlier this week, losing the $3.10–$3.12 support zone that had underpinned bullish setups since late 2023. Price action has also dropped beneath the 20, 50, 100, and 200-day exponential moving averages, now clustered between $3.38 and $3.50. The RSI (14) has declined to 36.99, suggesting a bearish momentum shift.

The market is now testing a support band between $3.05 and $2.95. This zone has historically attracted dip buyers, but failure to hold may expose downside toward $2.75 or even $2.60. Traders are watching for any signs of a reclaim above $3.30–$3.35 as a potential reversal zone, though without structural improvement or fresh catalysts, rebounds are expected to be shallow.

Supply and demand imbalance favors sellers

July’s Lower 48 production has climbed to a record 107.2 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd), surpassing the June high of 106.4 bcfd. This has kept storage levels 6% above seasonal norms, reducing the urgency for aggressive near-term buying.

While LNG exports remain strong, currently around 15.8 bcfd—the gains have not been enough to absorb the excess supply. Weather forecasts through August 6 show above-average temperatures, but the revised outlook now anticipates less intense heatwaves, thereby reducing expected gas-fired cooling demand.

Our earlier coverage emphasized the risk of technical failure if $3.10 broke, as well as the importance of revised weather inputs. Both conditions have played out, with natural gas now in a vulnerable position unless supply slows or extreme heat returns to shift demand dynamics quickly.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Double-Checking The Credit Rating (Part 8): Public Storage
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today's article is about a leading company in the warehouse sector, part of the S&P 500. The company is distinguished by its large asset base and stable financial indicators. We talk about Public Storage (NYSE:PSA), its credit rating, and its financial instruments.

Company Overview

Public Storage, a part of the S&P 500 Index, is primarily focused on acquiring, developing, owning, and operating self-storage facilities. The company owns and operates 3,399 storage facilities in 40 states in the United States, with a total leased area of approximately 247 million square feet. The company was founded in 1972, and it is headquartered in Glendale, California. It owns a 35% interest in the common stock of Shurgard Self Storage Limited, which operates 318 storage facilities in 7 European countries. PSA's current market capitalization is close to $51.2 billion, and the total assets were approximately $19.6 billion at the end of the first quarter of 2025. The common stock of the company is now traded at a market price of $282.62:

PSA has an asset coverage ratio of 208%. It generates approximately 22.5% gross from self-storage facilities. Operating expenses (excluding depreciation) amount to approximately 29.44% of total revenues. The interest expenses are around 3% of the debt. When calculating the asset yield (by dividing total revenues by the value of assets), a value of 24% is obtained. However, for a more accurate assessment, a market adjustment is applied using the price-to-book value (P/B) ratio, which is currently 9.74. After this market adjustment, the calculated asset yield reaches 4.55%, and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio increases to approximately 1104%, which is an extremely high ratio, perhaps the highest in the sector. The forward P/FFO is 13.30, so the expected FFO yield comes to 5.8%. PSA has been rated by S&P with a credit rating of A.

Preferred stocks

PSA has all 14 preferred stocks:

All of them are rated by S&P with BBB+, and all of them are rated with an A3 credit rating by Moody's. All fourteen are traded below par. Their current yield range is from 6.21% to 6.34% and their current stripped yield range is from 6.24% to 6.36%. (NYSE:PSA.PR.F), (NYSE:PSA.PR.G), (NYSE:PSA.PR.H), (NYSE:PSA.PR.I), (NYSE:PSA.PR.J), and (NYSE:PSA.PR.K) call dates have passed, so they are callable anytime. (NYSE:PSA.PR.L), (NYSE:PSA.PR.M), (NYSE:PSA.PR.N), (PSA.PR.O), (NYSE:PSA.PR.P), (NYSE:PSA.PR.Q), (NYSE:PSA.PR.R), and (NYSE:PSA.PR.S) have years to call from 0.06 to 1.45. You can see all this info and more details above in the picture.

And here are the metrics of the stocks:

PSA.PR.F is priced at a market price of $20.72 currently. Its current yield is 6.21%, and its current stripped yield is 6.24%. It pays cumulative distributions of 5.15% annual amount, or $1.29 per annum, in the form of $0.321875 quarterly. PSA issued 10 million shares from the stock on 05/23/2017.

At the time of writing, PSA.PR.G has a current yield of 6.19% and a current price of $20.31. It pays cumulative distributions of 5.05% annual amount, or $1.26 per annum, in the form of $0.315625 quarterly. PSA issued 12 million shares from the stock on 07/31/2017.

PSA.PR.H is priced at $22.90, and its current yield is 6.10%. It pays cumulative distributions of 5.60% annually, or $1.40, in the form of $0.35 per quarter. PSA issued 11.4 million shares from the stock on 02/27/2019.

PSA.PR.I is with a current price of $19.33 and a current yield of 6.3%. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 4.88% annual amount, or $1.22 per annum, in the form of $0.306875 per quarter. PSA issued 11 million shares from the stock on 09/05/2019.

PSA.PR.J is priced at $18.59. Its current yield is 6.34%. It pays cumulative distributions of 4.70% annually, or $1.18, in the form of $0.29375 per quarter. PSA issued 9 million shares from the stock on 11/05/2019.

PSA.PR.K is with a price of $18.74 at the moment and a current yield of 6.35%. It pays cumulative distributions of 4.75% annual amount, or $1.19 per annum, in the form of $0.296785 quarterly. PSA issued 8 million shares from the stock on 12/11/2019.

Currently, PSA.PR.L is trading at a price of $18.34, and its current yield is 6.29%. It pays 4.625% cumulative distributions, or $1.15625 per annum, in the form of $0.2890625 quarterly. PSA issued 20 million shares from the stock on 06/08/2020.

PSA.PR.M is with a current price of $16.33 and a current yield of 6.34%. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 4.13% annual amount, or $1.03125 per annum, in the form of $0.2578125 per quarter. PSA issued 8 million shares from the stock on 08/11/2020.

PSA.PR.N is priced at $15.28 at the time of writing. Its current yield is 6.34%. It pays cumulative distributions of 3.875% per annum, $0.96875, in the form of $0.2421875 per quarter. PSA issued 10 million shares from the stock on 09/29/2020.

Currently, PSA.PR.O is trading at a price of $15.41, and its current yield is 6.32%. It pays 3.90% cumulative distributions, or $0.975 per annum, in the form of $0.24375 quarterly. PSA issued 6 million shares from the stock on 11/09/2020.

PSA.PR.P is with a price of $15.82 at the moment and a current yield of 6.31%. It pays cumulative distributions of 4.00% annual amount, or $1 per annum, in the form of $0.25 quarterly. PSA issued 21 million shares from the stock on 06/07/2021.

PSA.PR.Q is priced $15.55. Its current yield is 6.34%. It pays cumulative distributions of 3.95% annually, or $0.9875, in the form of $0.246875 per quarter. PSA issued 5 million shares from the stock on 08/11/2021.

PSA.PR.R is with a market price of $15.81 and a current yield of 6.32%. It pays cumulative distributions of 4% per annum, or $1, in the form of $0.25 per quarter. PSA issued 17.4 million shares of the stock on 11/09/2021.

PSA.PR.S is priced $16.18. Its current yield is 6.34%. It pays a cumulative distribution of 4.10% per annum, $1.025, in the form of $0.25625 per quarter. PSA issued 10 million shares of the stock on 01/04/2022.

OTC Bonds

Below in the chart are PSA's unsecured bonds.

All of them are rated with a credit rating of A2 by Moody's. Their yield range is from 3% to 5.76%. The maturity range is from 1 year to 28 years.

Credit rating

As our readers know, we use Moody's approach to track companies' credit ratings. We remind you of what it looks like in the picture below:

1. Total assets: The company has assets worth approximately $19.6 billion. According to Moody’s methodology, this is classified as Aa3.

2. Unencumbered assets: The unencumbered assets are around 86% of the gross assets. It receives an A3 equivalent credit rating.

3. Secured debt: The level of secured debt is classified as Aaa.

4. Total debt+preferred stock to total assets: A ratio of around 44.5% results in a Baa3 rating.

5. Net debt to EBITDA: With EBITDA of $3.34 billion and a net debt/EBITDA ratio close to 4, this metric receives a rating of Baa1.

6. Fixed charge coverage: EBITDA covers interest expenses and the preferred dividend close to 6 and a half times, and this ratio leads to an A1 rating.

The overall score of these indicators is 5.93, which corresponds to an A2 rating. After correction, the score is 4.26, which is equivalent to Aa3.

Market Positioning and Asset Quality

Here, PSA receives an A2 credit rating.

Operating environment

Same here; the rate is A2.

Liquidity and access to capital

The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are about 29.44% of revenue. EBITDA barely covers interest and dividends on preferred shares 6.8 times. Based on these indicators, all other things being equal, PSA deserves a credit rating of A1.

After calculating a score for market positioning, operating environment, and liquidity and adjusting all ratios based on the market-adjusted characteristics, we get a credit score of 4.26, or a solid Aa3 credit score.

Conclusion

Public Storage demonstrates strong financial stability and market positioning. With high asset coverage, stable revenues, and excellent liquidity, the company is deservedly awarded an Aa3 credit rating, placing it among the most solid companies in the REIT sector.

data.title: Alphabet: Nobody Adds $10B In CapEx If Business Is Slow (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOGL) reported Q2 earnings after the bell, surprising the market with better-than-expected numbers and also confirming that AI is driving the gains (and higher spend). CEO Sundar Pichai kicked off the earnings call by stating: "AI is positively impacting every part of the business, driving strong momentum.” Google isn't the loudest AI play in the room and investors should be taking advantage of that fact, especially with the stock trading comfortably below its fair value of $228.67, currently at $191, up almost 4% in pre-market.

Here’s the good news:

  • Search revenue hit double-digit growth, up to $54.2B, comfortably ahead of estimates for $52.7B, hushing any doubts about Google’s search engine dominance, particularly with OpenAI AI-powered browser prepping for release, and confirming that the new AI search features, including AI mode (launched in the U.S., and India has 100M users) and AI Overview (serves +1.5B users/month) are paying off.

  • YouTube ad revenue also outperformed for the quarter, growing to $9.8B versus estimates of $9.5B. Notably, YouTube shorts now earn “as much revenue per watch hour as traditional in-stream on YouTube.”

  • Cloud is running at an annual run rate of +$50B with Google Cloud revenue growing 32% to $13.6B, ahead of expectations for $13.1B and backlog grew 18% Q/Q and 38% Y/Y to $106B with a number of deals +$250M, doubling Y/Y.

  • Gemini expansion continues, with the Gemini app now boasting over 450 M monthly active users, and engagement is growing 50% from last quarter.

What we like about Google is that the market wasn’t really expecting much from the company, even on the AI front; all we’ve gotten in headlines is concern over OpenAI’s AI-powdered browsers pressuring Google Search and regulatory antitrust risk. The reality is that we’ve seen disciplined execution from management; nevertheless, Google has never received the same level of support as other Mag7 players. The stock is holding steady on the YTD chart, up less than 1%.

This works to our benefit as investors, as it makes for easier outperformance against expectations, as shown in the chart below. Google, in our opinion, alongside Meta (META), will have the easiest time integrating AI into their business for commercial use; Overview AI and AI mode are already proving that, as are the 50M users on Google Meets using AI-powered meeting notes or the 9M developers building with Gemini or the +70M videos produced with Veo 3 AI model. Take your pick, all point to the fact that these use cases are trickling back into higher Google search queries.

Not to mention, with the ASIC race heating up heading into next year, Google is extremely comfortable in its shoes. Google TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) are the only custom-design hardware accelerators that are already operational for machine learning workloads, although they’re workload is limited to stuff like checking Gmail content. Even OpenAI held some early tests for Google TPUs earlier this year; for now, OpenAI doesn’t have plans to deploy the chips at scale and will continue to rely on Nvidia GPUs and those of AMD, but that is likely to change down the line as ASIC becomes the industry go-to for low to mid complexity inference. In spite of no current plan for OpenAI to use Google’s TPUs, the consideration in itself is a massive validation of Google’s credentials. The Digitimes report published this Monday emphasizes that:

Among the top-tier clients, Google and AWS are currently the most proactive in both initiating chip designs and placing procurement orders.

To add, Digitimes’ 2024 report on the ASIC market share noted that Google was expected to hold a 74% market share last year. The company is working on Ironwood, the 7th generation TPU, designed to power AI inference workloads, including new video, image and speech and even music gen AI tools via Vertex AI. TPU x5 and Vertex AI pricing also undercut that of Amazon (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure. We like where Google TPUs are headed and think investors should recognize the upside that can come through solid footing on this front, come 2026, and Google’s ability to cut down costs, including its growing CapEx, which we’ll discuss next.

No one spends $10B extra in CapEx cause business is slow

The real surprise this quarter was Google upping its 2025 CapEx by about $10B from $75B to $85B, following the footsteps of Meta Platforms, which raised its CapEx outlook last quarter from $64B and $72B, up from the $60B to $65B.

The stock initially traded down on the CapEx increase because spend tends to scare investors. What we think calmed things down is the logical argument that, while yes, Google is spending more, it’s also seeing a return on those investments. This isn’t like Microsoft (MSFT) Co-pilot when we were all biting our nails waiting to see massive double-digit returns from the commercialization of AI. Google’s search engine has made the AI returns much more tangible to investors and the financials.

Management noted that the

“updated outlook reflects additional investment in servers, the timing of delivery of servers, and an acceleration in the pace of data center construction, primarily to meet cloud customer demand.”

The company has already spent $39.6B and now has $45.4B left to reach its target of $85B. We shouldn’t get too excited about seeing another CapEx increase into the 2H25 after this round. Speaking of 2H25, we also don’t think investors should expect substantially higher Y/Y growth in 2H25 due to the tough comparison from a year ago quarter when we saw high impact from the U.S. presidential election. We’re eyeing how 2026 CapEx will fare compared to this year, considering that the current year's CapEx is up over 61.83% from last year.

Google is one of the big spenders on AI for the year; if they were not spending, they wouldn’t be able to stay competitive, particularly with their recent push to integrate Gemini into apps. This is how management split their CapEx this quarter:

“The vast majority of our CapEx was invested in technical infrastructure, with approximately 2/3 of investments in servers and 1/3 in data centers and networking equipment.”

It's all about AI, and that's a good thing because it's already paying off.

Secret weapon: Waymo

Google’s other Bets segment doesn’t get enough love, and that’s not too surprising, considering the segment makes up $373 million out of $96,428M revenue, missing estimates for $429.1M, and runs at an operating loss of $1.2B. We’re more interested in this segment since Waymo has started to capture the Robotaxi market share. Waymo continues to scale, launching in Atlanta, doubling its Austin service territory, and scaling in Los Angeles and the San Francisco Bay Area by 50%. Management noted that:

The Waymo driver has now autonomously driven over 100 million miles on public roads. And the team is testing across more than 10 cities this year, including New York and Philadelphia.

Investors, particularly Tesla (TSLA) bulls, are having a hard time digesting the fact that Waymo could be the future of autonomous taxis, not Tesla. It’s a hard pill to swallow but it is what all data points indicate now from Waymo’s superior safety precautious using 13 RGB cameras, four LiDAR, and six external speakers for passenger interaction and gaining regulatory approval to scale into new cities, aiming for 10 cities in the near future while Tesla robotaxis remain stuck in the 10 vehicle Sunday launch.

Valuation & What to do with the stock

The Mag7 is kinda old news this year, but nonetheless, if we do look at Mag7 EV/Sales over the past three months, Google’s multiples are among the most attractive, growing at the lowest rate among peers with the exception of Apple that rose just 4.59% in contrast Meta (+37.8%), Microsoft (+35.6%), Tesla (+33.9%), Amazon (+27%), and Nvidia (+46.2%). Google’s forward P/E ratio is around 18.88x, also trailing peers with Microsoft at 33.03x and Amazon at 33.31x.

Even if we compare Google to the global ad peer group or the global tech group, it emerges as the most attractive based on multiples, as shown below. The stock trades below both group averages.

Google is a long-term play in our book. It is up roughly 7% since our last buy call earlier this month on the OpenAI vs Google Chrome debate, as shown below.

We believe Alphabet stock remains undervalued and recommend that investors seeking a low-risk, high-reward position with AI exposure consider adding Google to their portfolio.
